sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 18, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: LEFT moves the blue tip cursor left on the top bar. The top bar is a slider/cursor mechanism. The right edge color (currently W) may represent the selected paint color.
- Goal model: Create a cross pattern matching the bottom-left purple dots: g-N-g / N-g-N / g-N-g
- Recent findings: The top bar cursor moves with LEFT/RIGHT. Need to understand the relationship between cursor position and right edge color.
- Plan: Move cursor to different positions and observe right edge color changes. Then try painting the grid.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 17 of about 36 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting observations:
1. RIGHT moves the cursor right, but the 3rd RIGHT didn't change anything (already at the rightmost position)
2. The right edge stayed W throughout - it doesn't change with cursor position
3. Looking at the cursor tip, it changed from `bbSS` to `SSbb` when moving right. The order of colors flipped!
   - Before: `bbSS` (blue-blue-sky-sky)
   - After RIGHT: `SSbb` (sky-sky-blue-blue)
   - After 2nd RIGHT: `SSbb` (same)
   - After 3rd RIGHT: `SSbb` (same, at rightmost)
Wait, looking more carefully:
- After 1st RIGHT: `...ggggggSSbbggBB` (SSbb with gg after)
- After 2nd RIGHT: `...ggggggSSbbBB` (SSbb, no gg after, at end)
- After 3rd RIGHT: same (already at end)
So the cursor can move right until it hits the end. The 3rd RIGHT was a no-op.
Now let me think about what the game is. The right edge is W and doesn't change with cursor position. Maybe the right edge is not a color selector but something else.
Let me reconsider the game. Looking at the initial state:
- Right edge was N (light green)
- Bottom-right grid had W cells in cross pattern and g cells elsewhere
- Top bar cursor was at some position
After my actions:
- Right edge became W
- Bottom-right grid is all g
- Top bar cursor moved around
Maybe the right edge represents the "target color" that needs to be painted on the grid. Initially it was N, meaning I needed to paint N cells. But after I started clicking, it changed to W.
Wait, maybe the right edge is a "remaining cells" counter or something. Let me count:
- Initially: N strip, 64 pixels tall (full height)
- Now: W strip, still 64 pixels tall
The color changed but the size didn't. So it's not a counter.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe:
- The right edge shows the current "active" color
- The top bar cursor selects which grid cell to affect
- Clicking (MOUSE) applies the color to the selected cell
But that doesn't explain why the grid cells don't persistently change.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the initial state. The initial bottom-right grid had:
- W cells at cross positions: (0,1), (1,0), (1,2), (2,1)
- g cells at corner/center positions: (0,0), (0,2), (1,1), (2,0), (2,2)
And the right edge was N. Maybe the game is:
- The right edge shows the color I need to paint
- The bottom-right grid shows the current state
- I need to paint the cross pattern with the color shown on the right edge
Initially, the right edge was N, and the cross cells were W. So maybe I need to change the W cells to N. But when I clicked them, they changed to N temporarily and then everything became g.
Hmm, let me try a different approach. Let me look at the animation frames to see what happens during clicks.
Actually, let me first check if the bottom-right grid cells have changed. The segmentation showed all 9 cells as g (gray). But maybe I need to look at the actual colors more carefully.
Let me also consider: maybe the game uses the top bar cursor to select WHICH cell to paint, and MOUSE clicks to paint. So:
1. Move cursor to select a cell position
2. Click to paint that cell with the color shown on the right edge
But the cursor is on the top bar, not on the grid. How does cursor position map to grid cells?
Wait, maybe the cursor position on the top bar corresponds to a column or row in the grid. The top bar has a certain width, and the cursor can move left/right. Maybe:
- Cursor at left → column 0
- Cursor at middle → column 1
- Cursor at right → column 2
And UP/DOWN moves select rows, while MOUSE clicks paint.
Let me test this theory. Let me try UP and DOWN to see if they affect something.
Actually, I just realized something. The right edge is a vertical strip. Maybe UP and DOWN move a cursor on the right edge, selecting different colors. And LEFT/RIGHT move the cursor on the top bar, selecting different grid positions.
Let me try UP and DOWN to see if the right edge changes.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: RIGHT moves cursor right on top bar (reached rightmost after 2 moves). The cursor tip colors flipped from bbSS to SSbb. Right edge stayed W. Need to understand the relationship between cursor, right edge, and grid painting.
Recent findings: Cursor tip orientation changes with direction. 3rd RIGHT was no-op (at limit). Right edge color doesn't change with cursor position.
Plan: Try UP/DOWN to see if they affect the right edge color strip. Also check if the cursor position maps to grid cells.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

# Check right edge full height
right_colors = []
for r in range(48, 64):
    right_colors.append(ascii_lines[r][63])
print(f"Right edge (rows 48-63): {''.join(right_colors)}")

# Try UP
result = action(['UP'])
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
right_colors = [ascii_lines[r][63] for r in range(48, 64)]
print(f"\nAfter UP: {''.join(right_colors)}")
print(f"Row 19: {ascii_lines[19][10:45]}")

# Try DOWN
result = action(['DOWN'])
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
right_colors = [ascii_lines[r][63] for r in range(48, 64)]
print(f"\nAfter DOWN: {''.join(right_colors)}")
print(f"Row 19: {ascii_lines[19][10:45]}")

# Try multiple UPs to see if right edge changes
for i in range(5):
    result = action(['UP'])
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
right_colors = [ascii_lines[r][63] for r in range(48, 64)]
print(f"\nAfter 5 UPs: {''.join(right_colors)}")
print(f"Row 19: {ascii_lines[19][10:45]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
